entry.title: Re: Homepage redesign customization level — SAP Signavio...
entry.content: Hi, if you are unsure if the design is compliant with SAP guidelines it might be good to open a compliance ticket. Otherwise, your team or your manager might be a good source to get further directions. But unless you do not need feedback from customers, the SAP Signavio community is the wrong place imho.

entry.title: Early Validation Gap
entry.content:
We ran into a situation during an ECC to S/4HANA migration where validation was technically “planned,” but practically happening too late.

Most issues only showed up during mock loads:

  • Business teams flagged mismatches late

  • Rework cycles increased

  • Timelines started slipping

What helped:

We shifted validation earlier into the process—before mock loads—by running smaller, iterative checks aligned with business rules.

This reduced surprises significantly and made business sign-off smoother.

Curious —

Are you validating continuously during migration, or still relying on mock cycles?

entry.title: Everything You Always Wanted to Know About InnerSource Adoption in Your Organization*
entry.content:
How widely is InnerSource really adopted in your organization - and what’s holding teams back? In this post, we explore how a well-designed, representative survey can cut through assumptions, reveal the true state of InnerSource adoption, and uncover the obstacles an InnerSource Program Office (ISPO) needs to address.

Why an InnerSource Survey Matters

InnerSource transformations do not succeed simply by promoting best practices or inspiring a handful of motivated teams - they succeed when the organization as a whole understands and embraces the model. Yet one of the biggest challenges for any InnerSource Program Office (ISPO) is understanding the real state of InnerSource adoption across the company.

By the nature of their work, ISPO’s and Open Source Program Offices (OSPO’s) primarily interact with people who are already familiar with Open Source principles or who are naturally enthusiastic about InnerSource. This might create an unintended “expert bubble”: a circle of colleagues who already understand and appreciate the value of transparency, reuse, and cross-team collaboration. While these individuals are essential champions, they might represent only a fraction of the overall organization. As a result, the ISPO’s perception of InnerSource maturity can easily become skewed - often appearing much more advanced than what the broader company reality reflects.

A structured, organization-wide InnerSource survey helps break out of this bubble. It provides the ISPO with an unbiased, data-driven view of what is actually happening.

Moving Beyond Assumptions

Without structured data, assessments of InnerSource adoption often depend on anecdotal evidence or impressions. A survey enables a realistic evaluation of awareness, participation, and perceived challenges.

Managers value measurable, verifiable information. Survey data enables the ISPO to communicate clearly and convincingly with leadership. Instead of narratives or isolated success stories, the ISPO can present factual insights about adoption levels, challenges, and opportunities. This not only strengthens the credibility of the InnerSource initiative but also helps justify budget, staffing, and strategic support.

Understanding Awareness, Barriers, and Communication Channels

A well-designed InnerSource survey goes far beyond asking who uses InnerSource. It helps uncover why people do - or do not – adopt InnerSource practices. For instance:

  • Are employees aware of what InnerSource is?
  • Do they know how to get started?
  • Do they feel confident contributing to other teams’ codebases?
  • Which organizational, technical, or skills-related barriers stand in the way?

These insights are critical. They allow the ISPO to tailor trainings, documentation, and enablement efforts to what teams actually need, not what the ISPO thinks they might need.

Effective communication is a cornerstone of any InnerSource rollout. But communication only works if people see it, understand it, and find it useful. A survey helps evaluate the ISPO’s communication channels: Are internal newsletters reaching developers? Is the documentation platform known and accessible? Are community calls perceived as valuable? These answers ensure that ISPO outreach is not only active, but impactful.

Data That Enables Strategic Focus

The insights gained from an InnerSource survey can provide an essential foundation for ISPO strategy and planning. A representative survey delivers quantitative data across different business units, countries, and locations, giving the ISPO a clear view of where InnerSource is thriving and where it is still largely unknown or unused. This level of granularity allows the ISPO to sharpen its focus and allocate efforts where they will have the greatest impact.

This enables:

  • Activity planning: Identifying which topics require more training, mentoring, or enablement.
  • Targeted focus: Understanding differences between units and regions helps the ISPO prioritize specific countries, locations, or organizational domains.
  • Performance management: Survey results form a baseline from which the ISPO can define meaningful goals and KPIs for itself.

With this data-driven foundation, the ISPO can move from broad, generic initiatives to precise, strategic actions that align with organizational needs and maximize value.

Designing an Effective InnerSource Survey

A well‑structured survey requires clarity about the target groups and objectives.

Separate Surveys for Developers and Managers

InnerSource adoption relies heavily on two distinct groups - developers and middle managers - each with very different responsibilities, perspectives, and challenges. Because their roles differ fundamentally, they require separate surveys tailored to their needs. SAP, therefore, runs two dedicated surveys – one targeting colleagues with development roles, one targeting middle managers (i.e., management levels L2, L3, …) - and alternate them each year: developers one year, managers the next.

Developers work directly with code, collaboration processes, and repositories, so they experience InnerSource at the most practical level. Their survey focuses on awareness, contribution behavior, and barriers that prevent participation. This provides insight into the day‑to‑day reality of InnerSource adoption.

Middle managers shape the environment in which InnerSource can succeed by defining priorities, resources, and team expectations. Their survey assesses support for InnerSource, perceived benefits, and organizational or structural blockers. This helps the ISPO understand leadership alignment and readiness across units.

Survey Structure

Every survey begins with the question: "Do you know what InnerSource is?". Since we cannot assume that every survey participant knows what InnerSource is, but we want to enable everyone to answer the remaining questions, we provide a brief explanation of InnerSource along with a short example.

The survey includes three sections:

  1. General Perception and ISPO Communication: This part collects insights into how InnerSource is perceived overall and how well ISPO communication channels are known and used.
  2. InnerSource Contributions: This section examines whether individuals or teams contribute to InnerSource projects and why contributions happen or do not happen.
  3. Application of InnerSource in Own Projects: Here the survey investigates whether teams use InnerSource practices in their own repositories, such as openness to contributions or the presence of contribution guidelines.

Free‑text fields allow respondents to provide nuance, examples, and concerns that predefined choices would miss. Although they require more evaluation effort, they often surface the most valuable insights.

To enable comparison of survey results across years, we keep the questions as stable as possible. This allows us to identify trends.

Each survey is sent to a randomized, representative group of colleagues. Responses remain anonymous, but include structural attributes such as location, country, organizational unit, and manager type (Manager Manages Teams / Manager Manages Managers – MMT/MMM) to support drill‑down analysis.

What We Learned and How SAP’s ISPO Responded

Running regular InnerSource surveys provides valuable insights - but the real impact comes from what an organization does with those insights. At SAP, the ISPO has used survey results not only to assess the current state of InnerSource adoption, but to define clear, targeted actions that address the most important challenges identified by developers and managers. Below, we present some of the key findings from SAP’s surveys and the initiatives derived from them.

High Perceived Value - but Low Awareness

One of the clearest survey signals was that both developers and managers strongly associate InnerSource with important benefits, such as increased developer efficiency, improved cross-team collaboration, and higher reuse of code and solutions.

At the same time, a surprisingly large share of respondents indicated that they do not know what InnerSource is. Free‑text answers reinforced this point: many participants held misconceptions about InnerSource or stated that they were “not familiar with the open source way of working.”

To address this gap between perceived value and actual understanding, the SAP ISPO launched several initiatives:

  • More training and education focused on practical InnerSource skills
  • Inclusion of InnerSource in internal curricula for developers, architects, and related roles
  • Sharing concrete examples through SAP-internal InnerSource patterns, use cases, and success stories (see [3])
  • Presentations and awareness sessions across units, locations, and communities

These efforts aim to ensure that the benefits people associate with InnerSource can actually be realized in practice.

Awareness Varies Significantly Across Locations

One of the most striking findings was the large difference in InnerSource awareness across SAP’s major development locations. Some regions showed comparatively high familiarity with InnerSource, while others had very low recognition of the concept.

To address these regional disparities, the ISPO is working on strengthening local engagement structures:

  • Connecting directly with local Managing Directors at development locations
  • Starting regional InnerSource communities to build local momentum
  • Introducing regional InnerSource ambassador programs to provide on‑the‑ground support and advocacy

These regional initiatives help tailor awareness activities to local cultures, priorities, and development ecosystems.

Developers Struggle to Find InnerSource Projects

A frequently mentioned barrier was the difficulty of discovering relevant InnerSource projects. Developers who were motivated to contribute often did not know where to find suitable projects or how to identify opportunities aligned with their skills.

To solve this, SAP launched the InnerSource Project Portal - a centralized internal platform listing all InnerSource projects (see [1], [5]). The portal allows contributors to browse projects by topic, technology, or domain, search for contribution opportunities, identify active InnerSource initiatives across the organization. This reduced one of the most practical barriers to participating in InnerSource.

Insufficient Incentives Were Seen as a Major Blocker

Another recurring theme was a perceived lack of incentives for InnerSource participation. Some respondents felt their contributions were not recognized.

To improve recognition and increase the visibility of InnerSource contributions, SAP is planning to introduce an InnerSource award. The award will highlight and acknowledge outstanding InnerSource projects, significant cross-team contributions, and sustained efforts to strengthen collaboration and knowledge sharing. Its purpose is to create positive visibility, encourage participation, and reinforce that InnerSource work is valued across the organization.

Conclusion

Well designed InnerSource surveys offer the data and insights required to uncover barriers, steer targeted improvements, and strengthen the effectiveness of an organization’s InnerSource initiatives. By translating survey results into targeted actions, ISPO's are able to directly address challenges.

Further Information

[1] InnerSource & Discoverability (InnerSource Commons Community Call, July 2021)

[2] Establishing InnerSource at SAP (SAP blog post, October 2021)

[3] Cultivating InnerSource at SAP (SAP blog post, January 2024)

[4] InnerSource Contribution Insights (Presentation at InnerSource Commons Summit, November 2025)

[5] InnerSource Portal (InnerSource Commons Pattern)

*But Maybe Were Afraid to Ask

entry.title: Re: Timesheet Time Entry with Multiple Activity Allocatio...
entry.content:
Hello @S0025758586

Apologies for the delayed response.

Wha did I mean by “copying” in Q2?

I meant the standard Copy Time Entries function in timesheet which lets you copy existing time entries from selected source days or weeks to target days or weeks. Your understanding is correct that in standard, one timesheet record is not automatically transformed into multiple activity allocations such as T001, T002 and T003 from the same 13:00 to 15:00 entry.

So, for your scenario, the practical standard options remain:

  • For you to create multiple time entries if you need multiple activity type postings, or
  • You use a periodic allocation approach for indirect costs rather than trying to derive several activity types from one timesheet source record.

You see D10, D20, D30 and not D110 in Overhead Calculation because Public Cloud uses simplified and predefined standard content in many controlling configuration areas. In other words, D110 is not configurable in Public Cloud.

Because of that, your observation is consistent with the behaviour in Public Cloud, which provides costing sheet logic, but the design is more restricted than on premise/private cloud, and basically not giving you that same flexibility for fine grained per WBS dependency logic or template driven derivation that you are looking for.

So, if your requirement is to derive several indirect cost components from one labour-based source entry and post them separately to WBS with project specific flexibility, then Public Cloud does not that in the same way as production activity confirmation scenarios. The better alternative is Universal Allocation, where you can allocate plan or actual values from sender to receiver.

Best regards

Chris

entry.title: Re: Questions About Display Options in the New SAP Succes...
entry.content:
@Joe0603 

The View Details might appear if you have table fields defined in your Goal Plan, such as Milestones or Metric Lookup Table. In the Legacy version these would show on the Other Details tab, whereas the Goal Details tab could be controlled via form-layout configuration. 

Now, the visibility of these fields is based on the field-permissions that the users have, although you can still use tab-permissions to control the visibility of the tabs.

Hope this will help, 

Regards, 

Jordi

entry.title: Re: Fund Management - MIR7 Parked documents Deletion
entry.content:
Follow the below steps

  • Identify all parked documents created before FM activation.
  • Decide whether they should be posted (with FM coding added) or deleted.
  • Use FBV2/FBV3 or Manage Parked Journal Entries app to process them.
  • For large volumes, raise a ticket with SAP to explore technical clean‑up options.

entry.title: Re: Multiple Purchase Organization for one plant
entry.content: You cannot assign two purchasing orgs to one plant in S/4HANA Public Cloud. The correct approach is to use purchasing groups, roles, and custom fields to separate processes within a single purchasing org. If absolute separation is required, you’d need to model two plants instead.

entry.title: Re: Week 4 : Use Joule Predictive Code Completion to Implement Determination
entry.content: Week 4 Challange is done . Package : ZGE295999

entry.title: Re: Year end Activities of MM
entry.content: Year‑end activities in SAP MM focus on closing open purchase orders, reconciling inventory, and ensuring valuation consistency. The key tasks include clearing GR/IR accounts, checking open POs and deliveries, reconciling stock values with FI, and carrying forward balances. These activities are coordinated with FI, CO, SD, PP, and PM to ensure a clean financial close.

entry.title: Re: RAP cl_csp_act_read_from_db has a bug
entry.content:
Hi,

we had the same problem and found the reason and a solution.
The duplicate entries in R_FLEXWORKFLOWINSTANCE are from the view R_SWWFLEXPROC. There, SWF_FLEX_TEXT_FILL was called without the filter TEXTTYPE = 'WF_SUBJECT', causing the texts of the sub-steps in the workflow definition to be read, which led to the duplicate rows.
The fix is described in Note 3685411.

entry.title: Re: SuccessFactors Performance (Forms): Option to disable...
entry.content:
Hi @JDLidl 

The ability to show section descriptions expanded by default is coming on 1H 2026 Release, with a new option in Form Template Settings > Expand Form Section Descriptions on First Opening (only applicable to the latest form experience).

Additionally, the Show More is replaced by an arrow that can be selected to expand/collapse the descriptions as needed.  This is already published on the What's New Viewer (Early Access) for SAP Partners since March 27th. The What's New Viewer general access will be available in April 3. 

Hope this will help, 

Regards, 

Jordi

entry.title: Receipt Management - Irritants to follow the receipts to do
entry.content:
Hello, thank you by advance for your help.

Since we deployed in SAP for purchases, we have a problem regarding receipts. The major problem is for the moment we haven't found a way to clearly identify for the users the reception he has to do on the month, on the year, and on all the purchase orders he has. And the problem regarding this is as no receipt is put on the purchase order, it is impossible to put an invoice on the purchase order, and so sometimes we are late to pay a supplier.

Can you give me some ideas on the topic? Are there views/apps that allow us to follow easily the receipts on a specific user or on a specific month? Have you found this problems with other clients before and do you know if there are some solutions?

We're very open to new ideas because for the moment we don't know how to manage it and it is a very big irritant for our users and also for our purchasing team that is completing the invoices.

Thanks a lot for your reply and have a good day.

Romane QUAGLIARINI.

entry.title: Re: VIM 7.5 ECC – MRKO Posting Does Not Finalize DP Workf...
entry.content:
SAP/OpenText do not support bypassing VIM’s posting framework. If you need MRKO, you must wrap it inside VIM’s posting classes so the DP document is updated and workflow closed. Otherwise, you will always end up with active workflows and broken analytics.
Having said that, the fix is not in MRKO itself, but in ensuring VIM’s DP finalization logic runs after MRKO. You need to call the VIM posting APIs to update BUS_OBJTYPE/KEY and close the workflow.

entry.title: Overview of Advanced Payment Management XSD Schema Functionality
entry.content:
Overview of Advanced Payment Management XSD Schema Functionality

Advanced Payment Management is an integrated Capability in SAP’s S/4HANA payment processing landscape. The primary objectives of Advanced Payment Management are payment centralization, exception processing, and payment standardization. Supports the in-house banking scenarios such as Payments On Behalf Of (POBO), intercompany payments, and cash pooling or Receipts on Behalf Of (ROBO). Payment file formatting is a key aspect of payment processing in Advanced Payment Management.

Advanced Payment Management (APM)  provides two options for generating payment files in ISO 20022 formats:

1) Usage of DMEEX framework

2) Embedded XSD-based framework

. The XSD schema  enables validation of the  generated messages , ensuring that the generated output adheres to the defined schema structure.

In this blog, I explain how the XSD framework operates in Advanced Payment Management, focusing on the processing mechanism and demonstrating how XSD-based output generation can be achieved without implementing custom BAdIs, enabling organizations to follow the clean core principles.

Background Information

The system automatically derives it as part of the standard conversion mechanism. The format generation  is based on XSD schemas, which can be accessed and maintained through configuration or via transaction code /PF1/XSD.

There are three options to make changes to the output formats :

  1. Modify the XSD yourself make usage of the schema conform mapping
  2. Get XSD Files from a house bank and make usage of the schema conform mapping
  3. Implementation of BAdI

Regarding point 1, modification of individual nodes can be achieved by removing them directly from the XSD schema. A valid XSD schema from the house bank or a standard XSD from the previously mentioned transaction can be used. The XSD schema can then be adjusted using a text editor, for example, Notepad or Notepad++, according to the required structure and uploaded into SAP as a new message type. ensure schema-compliant mappings

Regarding point 2, you can get the bank specific XSD and upload it in APM using transaction code /PF1/XSD. You can then generate the payment XML file based on the bank’s XSD specification. ensure schema-compliant mappings

Regarding point 3, a BADI implementation needs to be created following the structure of the existing implementations, which can be accessed through Goto → BADI’s. This approach requires a basic level of development knowledge, although the effort involved is limited. In many cases, the existing standard implementation classes can be reused. The advantage of using these classes is that they ensure schema-compliant mappings, meaning that the generated XML output strictly follows the structure defined in the XSD schema.

If implementing a BAdI is not preferred, an alternative approach can be considered. In this case, the XSD provided by the house bank can be imported directly, or a standard SAP XSD can be downloaded and modified using tools such as XSD tool, XML tool, Notepad++ or Notepad to align with the bank’s requirements and the specific business needs. It is important to note that the XML output is strictly controlled by the XSD schema definition. Therefore, even if additional fields or tags exist in the payment order or item data (for example, tags that are visible in transaction /PF1/PO_EXPERT), they will not appear in the generated XML output unless they are explicitly defined in the XSD schema.

Finally, after preparing the XSD and message type, a new outbound converter must be created in configuration. The newly created message type should then be assigned in the Define XML Settings for Format Converters customizing step to ensure the correct schema is used during the output generation process.

DMEEX converter

In the DMEEX converter integration, the converter is automatically executed once the DMEE flag is enabled in the converter customizing overview. After activating this flag, it is not necessary to explicitly define a conversion program, as the system automatically determines the conversion class /PF1/CL_OPM_P_DMEEX_PAYM_CONV.  Please see the screenshots below showing the definition of the outgoing converter.

When using the DMEEX framework, the system uses  the relevant DMEE format definition to generate the payment output. However, it is important to note that the mapping within the DMEE tree is typically based on FPAY* structures, which are used in traditional SAP payment programs. In contrast, APM stores its payment data in /PF1/DB* tables, which requires an additional mapping layer between the APM data structures and the DMEE tree. The class /PF1/CL_OPM_P_DMEEX_PAYM_CONV handles this mapping by attempting to call the appropriate tree-specific mapping classes, such as /PF1/CL_DMEEX_OUT_CGI_CT, which convert APM data into the structures expected by the DMEE tree nodes. However, SAP does not provide mapping implementations for all available DMEE trees. Therefore, additional customizing options are available, which allow fields in the DMEE tree to be populated even if they are not currently mapped by the standard implementation, provided the relevant data exists in the APM data model.

In scenarios where conditions are defined within the DMEE tree, it is advisable to review whether an APM-specific DMEE tree should be created and whether the conditions can be removed. (Conditions in DMEEX are not fully supported in APM.)  When condition-based logic is used in the DMEE tree, certain XML elements may not appear in the generated output if the defined conditions are not fulfilled. There is additional customizing available as mentioned in Help.sap

Understanding XML Schema Definition (XSD) Files

In this section, I will give a brief overview of XML Schema Definition (XSD) files.

XML Schema Definition (XSD) files define the structure of an XML document by specifying the permitted elements and attributes. More specifically, XSD schemas describe the XML document structure, including syntax rules, data types, inheritance relationships, and validation constraints. An XML document may reference definitions from one or multiple XSD files, depending on the complexity of the schema. Similarly, an XSD schema can import or include definitions from other XSD schemas, enabling modular schema design. XML parsers use XSD schemas to validate XML documents against the defined structure and rules. In addition, many XML editors utilize XSD schemas to provide features such as auto-completion and schema-based validation, which significantly simplifies the process of creating and editing XML files.

Element types

In an XML document, each element is associated with a data type, which may be either a standard built-in type or a custom user-defined type. Elements may also include attributes, which provide additional metadata or characteristics related to the element.

Root Element

In XML, a document must contain a single root element. This root element acts as the main container that includes all other elements within the XML structure. For instance, in XSD schema files, the root element is typically <xs:schema>.

Namespace

Just like data types in software systems, types in XML schema are defined within a specific namespace context. To introduce a new namespace in the schema, it must be explicitly declared in the schema definition.

Simple element types

XML elements are categorized as simple or complex. A simple element is a basic element that holds a value but does not contain any child or nested elements.

Complex element types

A complex element may include child elements and/or attributes, whereas a simple element contains only a single value without nested elements. The rest of this section will focus on the structure and usage of complex elements.

Simple Type

A simple data type represents a value consisting of text or numeric content, without any nested or child elements. It defines a data type that stores a single value without any nested elements or associated attributes.

 Elements inside elements

A complex element may contain additional child elements. For instance, the GrpHdr element type consists of elements defined by the GroupHeader32 structure.

<xs:complexType name="GroupHeader32">
   <xs:sequence>
      <xs:element name="MsgId" type="Max35Text"/>
      <xs:element name="CreDtTm" type="ISODateTime"/>
      <xs:element maxOccurs="2" minOccurs="0" name="Authstn" type="Authorisation1Choice"/>
      <xs:element name="NbOfTxs" type="Max15NumericText"/>
      <xs:element maxOccurs="1" minOccurs="0" name="CtrlSum" type="DecimalNumber"/>
      <xs:element name="InitgPty" type="PartyIdentification32"/>
      <xs:element maxOccurs="1" minOccurs="0" name="FwdgAgt" type="BranchAndFinancialInstitutionIdentification4"/>
   </xs:sequence>
</xs:complexType>

Element indicators

Indicators define the permitted behavior and structure of the elements contained within an XML schema. In this section, only the indicators that are commonly used in BOE XSD schemas are described. Examples are provided below for reference.

  • All
    The child elements defined within this indicator may appear in any order. Each element can occur zero or once, but it cannot appear more than once.
  • Any
    This indicator allows the inclusion of elements that are not explicitly defined in the schema, providing flexibility to add additional elements beyond those specified in the schema definition.
  • Choice
    Only one of the specified child elements is allowed to appear. Although this definition is accurate, it may be misleading without further clarification, as shown in the example below.
  • Sequence
    The elements within this structure must appear in the defined order. While this definition is accurate, it may be misleading without further clarification, as demonstrated in the example below. To control the number of occurrences of an element, a sequence can be created containing only one element.
  • MaxOccurs/ MinOccurs
    It determines the allowed number of occurrences of an element, including both the minimum and maximum limits.

Restricting allowed values for elements

  • The previous section outlined how element behavior can be controlled, such as specifying how many times an element may appear and the order in which elements must occur. XML schema also allows restrictions to be applied to the permitted values of elements. The following section focuses on the restrictions most used in BOE XSD schemas, with examples provided below.
  • Enumeration This restriction ensures that the element value must be selected from a predefined list of enumerated values, allowing only one of the specified options to be used.
  • Pattern Comparable to the use of regular expressions in Perl. This restriction ensures that the element’s value follows a specific pattern.
  • MinLength /MaxLength This restriction determines the minimum and maximum number of characters permitted for values of elements of this type.
  • MinOccurs / MaxOccurs This restriction determines the minimum and maximum permissible values for elements of this type, including the defined boundary values.
  • Choice This defines a group of alternative elements in which only one element may occur at a time, and the presence of multiple elements is not permitted.
  • Name This represents the identifier assigned to an element, attribute, or data type within the XML schema definition.

Use Case

In our example, the payment order data contains structured remittance information. Because the converter maps all data that complies with the defined schema, the remittance details are processed and generated correctly in the output file.

Important is that the output convertor map schema compliant and the generated format is lack validated. Validated can be switched off in the convertor XML attributes schema compliant mapping cannot be switched off.

For this blog, only simple adjustments such as those required by a bank should be considered. For this approach, the following prerequisites apply:

  • A valid XML Schema Definition (.xsd) file must be available that describes the required payment format or a related business object (for example, a recall message).
  • The XSD file must be accessible on the presentation server (local PC) so that it can be uploaded and maintained within Advanced Payment Management.

Standard Settings

The defined XML Schema Definitions (XSDs) can be used in the Incoming Payment Order (IPO) and Outgoing Payment Order (OPO) XML Format Converters for processing and generating XML messages. You can use XML schemas as follows:

  • As a base template for implementing an XML converter. For additional details, refer to the relevant SAP documentation on Message Identifier for an XML Converter.
  • As a rule set for an individual schema validation of XML files. For additional details, refer to the relevant SAP documentation on  Identifier of an XML message that defines an XML schema definition (XSD).

With the Advanced Payment Management functionality, predefined XML schema definitions are provided for the converters that are already implemented. If necessary, these schemas can be modified by uploading adjusted versions. The customizing table that stores these schemas is safeguarded from SAP updates, ensuring that existing entries are not overwritten. SAP is only permitted to add new entries to this table.

To restrict the scope, as implementing such changes typically requires a solid understanding of XSD structures and expressions, the approach here will be to take an existing schema and modify it.  For example, by adjusting validation rules so that certain elements are either restricted or defined as mandatory. The discussion should remain limited to this level of modification. Going into detailed explanations of all XSD components and expressions would require deeper training on XSD design and schema handling and is beyond the scope of this blog.

Note: Within Advanced Payment Management, the full set of XSD capabilities is not supported. The framework allows only limited schema adjustments, such as tag deletion, element modification, and the definition of constant values.

XSD Schema validation  

Use Case: Validation file against the schema

The file is validated against the ISO 20022 XML format. If any incorrect or invalid tag is present in the XML structure, the bank will reject the file.

To get to the schema validation functionality within in Advanced Payment management configuration, follow the path Financial Supply Chain Management -> Advanced Payment Management -> External Interfaces -> File Handler -> Basic Configuration -> Set Up Converter -> XML Framework -> Set Up XML Schema Validation. Transaction /PF1/XSD

After following the mentioned configuration path, the screen below is displayed.

Press the Test Schema Validation  button to get to the screen below.

In the File Name field, select the XML schema to be uploaded to SAP.  Specify the message identifier in the XML Message Identifier field.

After pressing the Execute icon ,the system displays the schema.

There are two ways to obtain a bank-specific format:

Option 1: Use the bank-provided XSD that defines their specific XML requirements.

Option 2: Modify the existing XSD based on the bank’s specifications and the business or bank’s requirements.

Case 1: Modification

I now show an example of how to modify the schema.

The business requirement is, based on the order type and converter ID, only the IBAN should be sent in the outgoing payment file instead of the account number. To meet this requirement, the modified XSD should allow only the IBAN field and not the account number. We will download the standard XSD schema /ISO_PAIN.001.001.03 and modify the tag DbtrAcct, which in the standard schema allows both IBAN and account number. In the modified XSD schema, the schema will be restricted to output the IBAN only. The modified XSD file must be saved with a new file name given to ensure that the standard XSD schema delivered by SAP is not overwritten.

Steps to Download XSD Schema

From the XML Schema Definition screen, double click on the schema to be downloaded and modified.

Click on the Download icon  and store the file on your desktop, as shown below.

Press the Save button to save the schema on your desktop. The extension on the file is .xsd.  The downloaded XSD should be renamed accordingly.

We can modify the XSD using a XSD tool, XML Tool, Notepad++, or Notepad.  Below I am using Notepad++. Once the XSD is opened in Notepad++, it will be displayed as shown in the screenshot below.

Use the Find option in Notepad++ to search for the DbtrAcct tag.

In the screenshot below, the DbtrAcct tag is highlighted.

As shown in the screenshot, the DbtrAcct tag is mapped to “CashAccount16”, but because “CashAccount16” is mapped to multiple other attributes further in the schema, we need to rename the “CashAccount16” to “ZCashAccount16”, because I do not want the change the value in “CashAccount16”, as it would impact other places in the schema I am modifying.

Now I rename “CashAccount16” to “ZCashAccount16” for the DbtrAcct tag only.

 Standard Section (before)

<xs:complexType name="CashAccount16">
     <xs:sequence>
        <xs:element name="Id" type="AccountIdentification4Choice"/>
        <xs:element maxOccurs="1" minOccurs="0" name="Tp" type="CashAccountType2"/>
        <xs:element maxOccurs="1" minOccurs="0" name="Ccy" type="ActiveOrHistoricCurrencyCode"/>
        <xs:element maxOccurs="1" minOccurs="0" name="Nm" type="Max70Text"/>
     </xs:sequence>
</xs:complexType>

Renamed Section with Modification (after)

     <xs:complexType name="ZCashAccount16">
        <xs:sequence>
          <xs:element name="Id" type="ZAccountIdentification4Choice"/> 
        </xs:sequence>   
</xs:complexType>

 Next, we need to copy from the “CashAccount16” section to a new “ZCashAccount16” section.

We also need to rename “AccountIdentification4Choice” to “ZAccountIdentification4Choice” because this account identification is used in other sections of the schema.

Note: When modifying the schema, you should rename the name space so as not to impact other sections of the schema.

If the additional tags are not needed, they can be removed or commented out.  In this case, the not needed tags were removed, which is a cleaner approach.

Standard Section (before)

<xs:complexType name="AccountIdentification4Choice">
     <xs:sequence>
       <xs:choice>
        <xs:element name="IBAN" type="IBAN2007Identifier"/>
        <xs:element name="Othr" type="GenericAccountIdentification1"/>
       </xs:choice>
    </xs:sequence>
</xs:complexType>

Renamed Section With modification (after)  

 <xs:complexType name="ZAccountIdentification4Choice">
                        <xs:sequence>
                           <xs:element name="IBAN"type="IBAN2007Identifier"/>
                        </xs:sequence>
            </xs:complexType>

 The screenshot below shows the updated section in Notepad++.

Next, I will upload the modified XSD into SAP by using the transaction code /PF1/XSD.

At transaction code /PF1/XSD, press the upload button   . The below popup will appear.

At the Filename(XSD) field, use the popup to select the modified file on the desktop.  The populated popup is shown below.

Click the    Check icon to validate the XML XSD Schema is consistent.

Press the Execute    icon.

Re-execute transaction code /PF1/XSD to view the changed scheme in SAP. The XSD changes are cross-client configuration that require a workbench transport.

Once the XSD modified is uploaded, we have only the IBAN tag available in the schema.  On the screenshot below, if you compare the Modified XSD (left) to the SAP Standard XSD (right), you will observe the differences only in that the DbtrAcct tag has changed. As the “complex type” of AccountIdentification4Choice was changed in the modified schema, other areas in the schema using this type will not be impacted. 

Next, I assign the modified XSD to the required outgoing converter, as shown below. When the payment file is generated, the format will be adjusted accordingly. This is done in the configuration following the menu path Financial Supply Chain Management-->Advanced Payment Management-->External Interfaces-->File Handler-->Basic Configuration-->XML Framework-->Define XML Settings for Format Converter.

Note the following points on this configuration:

  • Do not assign the custom XSD in the field “XML Converter Message Identifier (MSGIDENT_CONV)”. This field should contain only the standard XSD.
  • The custom XSD should be assigned only in the field “XML Message Identifier (MSGIDENT_SCHEMA)”.
  • The custom XSD should be assigned in Maintain Multi-Bank Connectivity Connector Outbound Settings.

Case:2: Deletion of fields in XSD

I will now show how fields in the XSD can be deleted.

In this example, the business user wants to delete the unwanted tags that are being sent from an incoming payment file and routed through Advanced Payment Management but should not be sent to the external bank.

In the screenshot below, the section to be deleted from the XSD schema is highlighted.

Select the XSD schema that you want to download.

Click on the Download icon    and store it on your desktop.

Click the Save button.

The downloaded XSD should be renamed accordingly.

We can modify the XSD with the help of the XSD tool, XML Tool, Notepad++, or Notepad. For our test case, I am using Notepad++.

Once opening the XSD in the Notepad++, it will be displayed as shown in the screenshot below.

Note above, the Prd tag is mapped to type TaxPeriod2. By using the Find option in Notepad++, search for the TaxPeriod2 tag.

We will delete the above-mentioned selected schema sections. Alternatively, we could comment them out.

<xs:complexType name="TaxRecord2"> (on schema tag It will be Deleted)
<xs:element maxOccurs="1" minOccurs="0" name="Prd" type="TaxPeriod2"/>
<xs:complexType name="TaxRecordDetails2">( on schema tag It will be Deleted)
<xs:element maxOccurs="1" minOccurs="0" name="Prd" type="TaxPeriod2"/>
(entire block It will be Deleted)
<xs:complexType name="TaxPeriod2">
<xs:sequence>
<xs:element maxOccurs="1" minOccurs="0" name="Yr" type="ISODate"/>
<xs:element maxOccurs="1" minOccurs="0" name="Tp" type="TaxRecordPeriod1Code"/>
<xs:element maxOccurs="1" minOccurs="0" name="FrToDt" type="DatePeriod2"/>
</xs:sequence>
</xs:complexType>

As per the screenshot above, the TaxPeriod2 and Prd tags have been deleted from the schema.

We will upload the XSD that we modified into SAP by using transaction code /PF1/XSD.

Please select Upload button   .  The popup will appear.

At the Filename (XSD) field, use the popup to select a file.

Click on the Check   icon to validate the XML XSD schema is consistent. The system displays the message “XML schema definition is consistent.”

With the modified XSD, we have only the IBAN tag used.  The XSD changes are cross-client, and, therefore, require a workbench transport.

Click on the Execute    icon.

After going to transaction code /PF1/XSD, it is observed that the uploaded renamed schema is available.  As mentioned, the XSD changes are cross-client, which requires a workbench transport.

 In the above screenshot, you can observe that the Prd tag is no longer part of the schema.

If you compare the modified XSD (left) to the SAP standard XSD (right), you observe the differences, and the Prd tag is not included in the modified XSD.

In Advanced Payment Management configuration, the modified XSD can be assigned to the required converter, and the system will generate the payment file according to the schema assigned.

Note the below points on this configuration.

  • Do not assign the custom XSD in the field “XML Converter Message Identifier (MSGIDENT_CONV)”. This field should contain only the standard XSD.
  • The custom XSD should be assigned only in the field “XML Message Identifier (MSGIDENT_SCHEMA)”.
  • The custom XSD should be assigned in Maintain Multi-Bank Connectivity Connector Outbound Settings.

 Conclusion
In this blog, I outlined how the Advanced Payment Management XSD framework works, focusing on the processing mechanism and demonstrating how XSD-based output generation can be achieved without implementing custom BAdIs, enabling organizations to follow the clean core principle.

entry.title: Re: Risk points for remove stage version content catalog
entry.content:
Dear @shankar2007 


The staged catalog version is architecturally required for SAP Commerce Cloud's multi-catalog system. Removing it breaks the intended content lifecycle model.

These are below risks involved in deleting the stage content catalog in hybris commerce cloud.

1. Content Management & Governance

All edits occur immediately in the live environment, eliminating the safety buffer for review and approval.
SAP Commerce Cloud workflows are designed to operate on staged catalogs before synchronization to online
Content cannot be reviewed or approved before going live, violating governance best practices
Inability to prepare content in advance: Cannot stage seasonal campaigns, product launches, or promotional content ahead of time

2. Business Continuity

High risk of production errors: Mistakes made by content editors immediately affect live customers
Customer experience impact: Errors, incomplete content, or test data could be visible to shoppers
No rollback capability: Without a staged version, reverting problematic changes becomes complex and time-consuming
24/7 operational risk: Any content update could disrupt the live storefront at any time

3. Development & Testing

No safe testing environment: Cannot test content changes, restrictions, or component configurations before production deployment
SmartEdit functionality severely impaired: SmartEdit is designed to work with staged catalogs for preview and editing
Customization validation: Custom components and templates cannot be tested safely before customer exposure

4. Data Loss:

Version control loss: No ability to maintain multiple versions of content for comparison or rollback
Audit trail gaps: Difficult to track what changed, when, and by whom without staged-to-online synchronization records
Data corruption risk: Direct production edits increase the likelihood of accidental data deletion or corruption
Synchronization history lost: Cannot leverage synchronization logs for troubleshooting or compliance

5. Operational Impact

Content editor workflow disruption: Editors lose the ability to work on draft content without affecting live site
Increased pressure on content teams: Every action has immediate production consequences
Training complexity: Staff must be trained to work directly in production with zero margin for error
Collaboration challenges: Multiple editors cannot safely work on related content simultaneously

6. Performance & Scalability

Cache invalidation complexity: Direct production changes require immediate cache clearing, potentially impacting site performance
Concurrent editing risks: Multiple users editing the same content in production increases conflict and error potential
No performance testing buffer: Cannot test performance impact of content changes before they go live.


Regards,
Prasanth

entry.title: Re: REFX-CLM-Public Cloud-Valuation contract -Transfer po...
entry.content:
Below are the workarounds for existing Contracts (already mapped to COB J4)

1) Allocation Cycles (CO → Cost Center Transfer)
Set up periodic allocation cycles in Controlling to transfer expenses from the COB object (J4) to the relevant cost centers. This is the closest equivalent to settlement in Public Cloud. You can automate this monthly so you don’t need to manually repost thousands of contracts.

2) Manual Reclass Postings (FI Journal Entries)
For one‑time corrections, use FI journal entries to move balances from COB J4 to cost centers. This is practical for smaller volumes or audit adjustments.

For future Contracts, adjust the asset class mapping so that RoU assets post directly to cost centers instead of COB J4. This ensures new valuation postings hit the right cost centers without needing allocations.

entry.title: Re: Required Automation Of Delivery & PGI Creation an...
entry.content:
Good day @Ashish13$

Conceptually, yes, that BAdI is the right kind of replacement spot for changing eWay Bill header or transport details. In Public Cloud however, you should only us it if it is actually exposed as a released extension point in your tenant through the Custom Logic app or the released extensibility inventory.

Also, that BAdI would only help with India eWay Bill data enrichment. It is not the mechanism to automate delivery creation, PGI, or billing. For India eWay Bill itself is managed through DRC.

Best regards

Chris

entry.title: Re: SWNCONFIG repeated notification mails daily basis ( extended notifications )
entry.content: I understand the concern—flooding inboxes with the same 'To-Do' list every morning can lead to users ignoring the notifications entirely. The 'Full' filter is designed as a safety net to ensure nothing falls through the cracks, but sending it daily is likely overkill. Instead of removing it, we can configure SWNCONFIG to behave more intelligently: Daily Delta: Users receive an email only for new tasks assigned to them that day. Weekly Full Sync: Every Monday morning (or Friday afternoon), the system sends a comprehensive list of all currently open tasks. This keeps the daily alerts relevant while providing a weekly 'clean-up' reminder. Does this schedule sound like a better balance for your users?

entry.title: Re: Fiori List Report: The "Process All" Dilemma — Parsing OData $filter in ABAP
entry.content:
@rammel_sapdev Good question,  Not 100% future-proof. The naming convention for table IDs in a Fiori Elements List Report has never changed so far, but we can't rule out that it might in the future (hopefully not). I didn't find a more elegant solution,  another option was to search the view for all sap.ui.mdc.Table objects, but that also retrieves other tables like those inside value help dialogs.

Probably we should investigate how it works the "export Excel" standard functionality in order to find a more "future-proof" approach avoiding the hardcode of ID

The parsing and all the business logic are entirely in the backend.
