entry.title: How to Create Custom DRF Outbound Implementation for Table T880 in SAP S/4HANA
entry.content:
Hi Experts,

I am working on Data Replication Framework (DRF) in SAP S/4HANA and have a requirement to replicate data from table T880 (Company master data) to an external system.

As per my understanding, T880 is not available as a standard DRF-supported business object.

I would like to understand the correct approach for this scenario:

  1. How can we define a custom DRF business object for table T880?

  2. What are the steps to create a custom outbound implementation (Z*)?

  3. How to implement and link the outbound ABAP class with DRF?

  4. How is replication triggered for such custom objects (change pointers or manual DRFOUT)?

  5. In real projects, is DRF recommended for configuration tables like T880, or is a custom interface a better approach?

Any guidance, documentation, or sample implementation would be highly appreciated.

Thanks in advance.

entry.title: CVI integration in S4 Hana conversion
entry.content:
In SAP S/4HANA, the concept of integrating customer and vendor master data into a single entity called the Business Partner is introduced. This allows the same person or company to act as both a customer and a vendor, reducing the complexity of data management. The Business Partner (BP) concept is central to CVI. During S4 HANA Conversion, SAP offers tools and transactions to ensure the master data consistency check along with configuration, monitoring, and execution of the CVI conversion.

Here, Steps 1-4 has to be done in ECC system as pre-requisites and Step 5 has to be done once SAP system is converted to S4 HANA. In this blog, we are focusing on Vendor Integration to Business Partner specifically from MM side. However, the source document attached at the end of the blog covers Customer Master Data Integration as well along with various error troubleshooting mechanisms.

Step 1 : Master Data Consistency Check

The Master Data Consistency Check via CVI_PRECHK transaction is a pre-conversion process in SAP's Master Data Governance (MDG) system that allows users to validate the consistency of customer or vendor master data records. This tool checks whether required fields are populated, ensuring there are no duplicate entries and ensuring that the data adheres to business rules. This allows users to take corrective actions before the CVI run in conversion projects.

1.1In transaction CVI_PRECHK, you need to select Customer or Vendor and enter Server group and Description and click execute.

1.2 Once the job is completed, you can see the logs successfully.

1.3 You can re-run the job by clicking on the red wheel icon and you can open the results of the job log by clicking on the Run ID number. Check and clear all the errors and re-run the job until the error becomes 0.

Step 2: Check Customizing Configurations

In SAP, CVI (Customer-Vendor Integration) customizing checks are essential to ensure that the Customer-Vendor Integration process is properly configured and working as expected. Here are the steps as follows:

2.1 Activate Creation of Post Processing Orders 

IMG -> Cross-Application Components -> General Application Functions -> Post processing Office -> Business Processes -> Activate Creation of Post processing Orders .

Activate Creation of Postprocessing Orders for component AP-MD and Business Processes CVI_01 (Customer > Business Partner) , CVI_02 (Vendor > Business Partner), CVI_03 (Business Partner > Customer) and CVI_04 (Vendor > Business Partner). Mark the checkbox to activate the CVI_01 and 02 entries ONLY before conversion.

2.2 Activate PPO requests for Platform Object 

IMG -> Cross-Application Components ->Master Data Synchronization -> Synchronization Control -> Synchronization Control -> Activate PPO Requests for Platform Objects in the Dialog 

Create a new entry for the synchronization object BP (Business Partner) and mark the checkbox “PPO Active”.

2.3 Activate Synchronization Options 

IMG -> Cross-Application Components -> Master Data Synchronization -> Synchronization Control -> Synchronization Control -> Synchronization Options 

For Business Partner, Customer and Vendor the following entries must exist.

IMG -> Cross-Application Components -> Master Data Synchronization -> Synchronization Control -> Synchronization Control -> Activate Synchronization Options.

Insert new entries for

  • Source Object Customer and Target Object BP (Activate the Checkbox)
  • Source Object Vendor and Target Object BP (Activate the Checkbox)
  • Source Object BP and Target Object Customer
  • Source Object BP and Target Object Vendor

Synchronization options can be maintained with transaction SM30, view MDSV_CTRL_OPT. The following entries must exist:

2.4 Define Number Ranges for Vendor Master 

IMG -> Logistics - General -> Business Partner -> Vendors -> Control -> Define Number Ranges for Vendor Master Records -> Intervals 

2.5 Assign Number Ranges to Account Groups (Vendor) 

IMG -> Logistics - General -> Business Partner -> Vendors -> Control -> Define Number Ranges for Vendor Master Records -> Intervals 

2.6 Define Number Ranges for Business Partner 

IMG -> Cross-Application Components -> SAP Business Partner -> Business Partner -> Basic Settings -> Number Ranges and Groupings -> Define Number Ranges . Here, External Indicator is set.

2.7 Define Groupings and Assign Number Ranges 

IMG -> Cross-Application Components -> SAP Business Partner -> Business Partner -> Basic Settings -> Number Ranges and Groupings -> Define Groupings and Assign Number Ranges 

2.8 Define Number Ranges (for Relationships) 

IMG -> Cross-Application Components -> SAP Business Partner -> Business Partner Relationships -> Basic Settings -> Define Number Ranges 

2.9 Vendor to BP 

IMG -> Cross-Application Components -> Master Data Synchronization -> Customer/Vendor Integration -> Business Partner Settings -> Settings for Vendor Integration -> Define BP Role for Direction Vendor to BP 

2.10 Number Assignment (Vendor to BP) 

IMG: Cross-Application Components -> Master Data Synchronization -> Customer/Vendor Integration -> Business Partner Settings -> Settings for Vendor Integration -> Field Assignment for Vendor Integration -> Assign Keys -> Define Number Assignment for Direction Vendor to BP. Maintain the Vendor Account Group and BP Grouping and the same number indicator is set.

In some cases, the customer and vendor account group has overlapping number ranges and hence maintaining the same number ranges in BP for both grouping will not be possible. In those cases, BP number will vary from the vendor or customer number range. As a result, from step 2.6, you cannot maintain external indicator in BP and from 2.10 you cannot maintain same number indicator as well.

2.11 Assign Contact Person

Although this step is covered by SD consultant in customer master section and this setup is not maintained separately for vendor contact persons, it is mandatory to know the configuration for MM consultant as well.

IMG -> Cross-Application Components -> Master Data Synchronization -> Customer/Vendor Integration -> Business Partner Settings -> Settings for Customer Integration ->Field Assignment for Customer Integration - > Assign Attributes -> Contact Person -> Activate Assignment of Contact Persons

IMG -> Cross-Application Components -> Master Data Synchronization -> Customer/Vendor Integration -> Business Partner Settings -> Settings for Customer Integration -> Field Assignment for Customer Integration -> Assign Attributes -> Contact Person -> Assign Department Numbers for Contact Persons

IMG -> Cross-Application Components -> Master Data Synchronization -> Customer/Vendor Integration -> Business Partner Settings -> Settings for Customer Integration -> Field Assignment for Customer Integration -> Assign Attributes -> Contact Person -> Assign Functions of Contact Person

IMG -> Cross-Application Components -> Master Data Synchronization -> Customer/Vendor Integration -> Business Partner Settings -> Settings for Customer Integration -> Field Assignment for Customer Integration -> Assign Attributes -> Contact Person -> Assign Authority of Contact Person

IMG -> Cross-Application Components -> Master Data Synchronization -> Customer/Vendor Integration -> Business Partner Settings -> Settings for Customer Integration -> Field Assignment for Customer Integration -> Assign Attributes -> Contact Person -> Assign VIP Indicator for Contact Person

2.12 Assign Industries 

IMG -> Cross-Application Components -> Master Data Synchronization -> Customer/Vendor Integration -> Business Partner Settings -> Settings for Vendor Integration -> Field Assignment for Vendor Integration -> Assign Attributes -> Assign Industries 

IMG -> Cross-Application Components -> SAP Business Partner -> Business Partner -> Organizations -> Maintain Industry Systems and Industries 

Step 3: Synchronization Process

In SAP, the Synchronization Cockpit for Customer-Vendor Integration (CVI) is a tool used to monitor and manage the synchronization or conversion process from traditional customer/vendor master data to the Business Partner (BP) data in the SAP S/4HANA system.

Using the synchronization cockpit, you can carry out all the steps for master data synchronization in an SAP system, especially:

  • Selection, preparation and starting of synchronization runs.
  • Monitoring of synchronization runs
  • Postprocessing of synchronization errors

To start the Synchronization Cockpit, you can call transaction MDS_LOAD_COCKPIT.

3.1 For vendor to BP conversion, select the synchronisation process in the left-hand corner. You can enter the selection criteria such as vendor account group or supplier number and click execute.

Note: Check if the synchronisation program has synchronisation scope as Vendor with Contact Person before proceeding. If the synchronisation scope is vendor without contact person, check the SAP source document to activate business functions or implement SAP notes based on your ECC and SP versions.

3.2 In the Monitor Tab, you can see the status of the job logs for the synchronization process. You should also activate the queue as shown in the image below.

3.3 You can view the error logs by selecting the line item and click on call PPO icon.

3.4 Analyse and correct the errors and re-run the synchronisation process.

You can see the entries in tables such as CVI_VEND_LINK , CVI_VEND_CT_LINK and BUT000 after successful synchronization.

Step 4: Completeness Check:

A Completeness Check in the context of Customer-Vendor Integration (CVI) in SAP refers to verifying that all necessary data required for a customer or vendor to be successfully synchronized into a Business Partner (BP) is available and accurate. This ensures that there are no missing or incomplete pieces of data that could result in issues during the synchronization process from traditional customer/vendor records to the new Business Partner model in SAP S/4HANA.

You can use the transaction CVI_COMPL_CHK for this purpose. Here, you can see the vendor and vendor contact persons that yet to be synchronised successfully. If the transaction is empty, then the synchronisation process is successful, and your work is complete.

Step 5: Post Conversion Process

After completing the Customer-Vendor Integration (CVI) conversion in SAP S/4HANA, there are several post-conversion steps that need to be taken.

5.1 Activate Creation of Post Processing Orders (Direction BP to Customer/Vendor) 

IMG -> Cross-Application Components -> General Application Functions -> Post processing Office -> Business Processes -> Activate Creation of Post processing Orders 

Activate Creation of Postprocessing Orders for component AP-MD and Business Processes CVI_03 (Business Partner > Customer) and CVI_04 (Business Partner > Vendor). Mark the checkbox to activate the entries. 

5.2 Activate Synchronization Options (Direction BP to Vendor) 

IMG -> Cross-Application Components -> Master Data Synchronization -> Synchronization Control -> Synchronization Control -> Activate Synchronization Options  

If not already done in preparation phase, insert new entries for Source Object BP and Target Object Vendor Activate the entry by marking the activation indicator checkbox. 

5.3 Set BP Role Category for Direction BP to Vendor 

IMG -> Cross-Application Components -> Master Data Synchronization -> Customer/Vendor Integration -> Business Partner Settings -> Settings for Vendor Integration -> Set BP Role Category for Direction BP to Vendor 

5.4 Number assignment (CVI-Target: Vendor) 

Suppose previously in ECC system before S4 HANA conversion, for BP grouping IMPO, we have maintained From and To range as 0000200000 and 0000299999 and NR Status is maintained as empty and along with External indicator is set.

After successful S4 HANA conversion, we need to maintain the To number and NR Status with the last record number of the BP account group and remove external indicator. Then, we need to create a new Number Range Interval with From and To range with immediate number from previous NR status and NO external indicator is set. Please check below images to understand thoroughly.

Changes to Old BP Number Range:

Creation and Assignment of New BP Number Range:

Using the below path, then assign the new number range to the BP grouping.

IMG -> Cross-Application Components -> SAP Business Partner -> Business Partner -> Basic Settings -> Number Ranges and Groupings -> Define Groupings and Assign Number Ranges 

IMG -> Cross-Application Components -> Master Data Synchronization -> Customer/Vendor Integration -> Business Partner Settings -> Settings for Vendor Integration -> Field Assignment for Vendor Integration -> Assign Keys -> Define Number Assignment for Direction BP to Vendor 

Set the Vendor Number range to external and Business Partner Number range as internal to ensure that newly created Vendor will have the same numbering as the corresponding Business Partner. Set the flag “Same number”. 

Again, in cases where vendor account group and BP grouping number range is not the same (in cases of overlap as mentioned in step 2.10), the same number indicator cannot be set and external indicator cannot be set in vendor number range.

After the step 5 is completed, you can create Business Partner with General, Customer and Vendor Role.

----------------------------------------    End of docuement ----------------------------------------------------

entry.title: New Overwrite Option for Story Template Imports in Content Store
entry.content:
Overview

In the latest release of 2H 2025, SuccessFactors has introduced a long-awaited level of control to the report Center import process: the ability to decide whether or not to overwrite an existing template during import of story report from content Store. Previously, importing a report means one thing that your existing version was automatically replaced, with no backup and no way to preserve customization. This often led to accidental loss of formatting, logical updates, or team specific modifications.

What’s changed

  1. Overwriting is now optional, not automatic: Previously, importing a report template overwrote the existing version by default. With the new update, the system does not overwrite unless you explicitly select the new “overwrite” option during import.
  2. New overwrite option provides administrators with great control and flexibility: If you choose to overwrite, the imported template replaces the existing version, even if another user has customized or renamed it.
  3. Importing without overwrite option selection: If you don’t choose to overwrite, the system only imports the story if it has never been imported before. If a story with same internal ID already exists, you will receive an error stating that same that the report with the same internal ID already present.

 Prerequisites:

You need to have role-based permission prerequisites administrator permissions > Manage dashboards/reports > Story admin permissions enabled to access the content store.

Step by step process:

  1. Import option in the report center( available only for story reports):

2.Choosing content store with list all the standard story report template provided by SAP:

3.After selecting the report which needs to be imported, there is a checkbox at the end that says, “selecting this checkbox will overwrite the previously imported Story”. This checkbox needs to be selected if you want to overwrite an existing standard story.

 

4. If the checkbox is not selected and the standard report template already exist in the report center, then system will through an error message stating standard report with same internal ID is already present.

BEST PRACTICES:

  1. Always duplicate existing template before overwriting. This creates a backup copy of the existing template and ensures that you can restore any changes made to the existing template.
  2. Overwriting options should be used when its truly needed. If the existing template contains valuable user-made changes, always consider before overwriting.
  3. Keep a proper naming convention to avoid confusions, for example –

                       SF_EMP_Pension_Overview_v2.0

                       SF_EMP_Pension_Overview_v2.0_Updated

This help track changes over time and revert back to older versions when needed.

Thanks for reading this blog post. I hope you liked the blog and this added a valuable insight to your learning journey. Feel free to share your comments.

entry.title: SAP for Energy and Utilities Conference 2026: Highlights for Oil, Gas, and Energy
entry.content:
In times of uncertainty, face-to-face industry connections matter more than ever. Across oil, gas, and energy, organizations are prioritizing energy security, supply chain resilience, infrastructure reliability, and operational stability.

The SAP for Energy and Utilities Conference is the leading platform for transformation in the oil, gas, and energy and utilities industries. From April 21st to 23rd, industry leaders, SAP experts and partners are meeting in Toulouse, France, a city combing innovation and technological advancements with the beautiful historic backdrop of "La ville rose".

At the conference, companies including Arabian Drilling, Harbour Energy, Ipiranga, MB Energy, Saudi Aramco, SOCAR Türkiye, TotalEnergies and many more, will share how they are using data and modern ERP platforms to strengthen operational resilience. These sessions will showcase how digital transformation is helping energy organizations respond to uncertainty while maintaining secure and reliable operations. View the full agenda here.

Aside from customer presentations, you will be able to hear from SAP experts and partners, engage in interactive microforums, and network with industry leaders from around the world. Here are some of the highlights for oil, gas, and energy:

The Oil, Gas, and Energy track spans the two main conference days and features real-life innovation stories from our customers and partners, and presentations from SAP experts.

  • Saudi Aramco - Protecting SAP Systems and Managing Vulnerabilities: Abdulrahman Naffaa, Cybersecurity Expert at Aramco shares knowledge to strengthen SAP environments through layered defense, secure configuration, and proactive vulnerability management. Gain practical insights into recognizing common system weaknesses, implementing continuous monitoring, and embedding security into every phase of system operation.
  • Harbour Energy - Driving Business Transformation in Energy & Utilities with the SAP Integrated Toolchain: Together with our partner, Tricentis, Harbour Energy will share how the company is enabling business transformation by adopting the SAP Integrated Toolchain across its SAP landscape,  highlighting how a modernized QA approach is helping Harbour Energy increase efficiency and reduce risk.
  • Ipiranga - The Importance of Root Cause Analysis in Digital Transformation: Mirela Siani, Technology & Transformation Director at Ipiranga will share how a clear understanding of business objectives, combined with a systematic approach to identifying obstacles, enables organizations to select digital transformation initiatives that truly drive meaningful change.
  • Arabian Drilling - Scaling Upstream Service Operations: Hussam Al Najjar, Head of ERP at Arabian Drilling, explores how they executed a greenfield transformation to SAP S/4HANA Private Cloud on RISE in just eight months. You will learn how the program delivered a clean core, cloud-scalable ERP and HXM landscape that is analytics-ready and aligned with the SAP AI vision. 
  • SOCAR - Cloud-Based Maintenance via Service and Asset Manager: SOCAR Türkiye is using Service and Asset Manager for 350 teams to manage and coordinate seamless and timely data flow. Around 10.000 corrective and preventive maintenance activities are fulfilled each week. Get an overview of the system, as well as insights to manage knowledge, streamline work, and valuable learned experience.
  • Creating Business Value for Oil, Gas and Energy with SAP Business AI: Kelly Boyer, AI Expert for Oil, Gas, and Energy at SAP, and Klaus Borkenstein, Head of Product Management for Oil, Gas & Energy at SAP explore how AI workflows are transforming oil and gas operations. This session will highlight the convergence of enterprise data, AI, and partner collaboration to unlock new opportunities for operational excellence across the energy sector.

  • Customer keynote: From Vision to Value - Insights for Shaping Energy’s Future with François Tête, TotalEnergies, Denis Maia, Sabesp, and Kim Maren Ekrutt, SAP. TotalEnergies is undergoing a profound multi energy transformation, targeting carbon neutrality and reshaping its portfolio around low carbon hydrocarbons and large scale renewable electricity, with major implications for investments and digital capabilities. Sabesp, the largest water distribution utility globally, has implemented a structured transformation framework built on clear strategic pillars, simultaneously managing over 160 projects, delivering tangible results within months. After sharing their current strategies, they will engage in a discussion together with Kim Ekrutt from SAP.
  • SAP Keynote: Innovations in Industries with SAP's Andre Bechtold, President Industries & Experiences and Torsten Welte, Head of ENR Industries. This keynote explores how the industry is moving beyond fragmented transformation initiatives towards integrated, scalable digital platforms that enable resilience, sustainability and speed. With SAP as a strategic digital backbone, the session highlights how data, automation and intelligent operations are turning ambition into execution.
  • Guest speaker: The Power of Persistence, Insights from Paralympian Aaron Phipps. Aaron Phipps is a triple-Paralympian and gold-medal Great Britain Wheelchair Rugby athlete who began his sporting journey after surviving meningitis at 15, which led to the amputation of his legs and most of his fingers. He became the first amputee to summit Kilimanjaro without assistance. His message of perseverance, energy, and endurance resonates deeply, and his strategies for achieving goals translate powerfully to both business and life.

Fueling the Future: Inside SAP for Oil, Gas, and Energy with Stephane Lauzon, Global VP and Head of Oil, Gas, and Energy at SAP and Stuart Barnes,  Principal EMS Advisor at Harbour Energy. Stephane will share the latest innovations in SAP Business Suite, cloud, and AI-driven applications designed to help companies balance operational excellence, profitability, and sustainability. From traditional hydrocarbons to new energy products, you will discover how SAP supports the industry in delivering reliable, affordable energy.

Stuart will give an overview of the company, insights into its growth journey, and its outlook for business transformation in 2026 and beyond, including how Harbour Energy is leveraging an integrated digital toolchain to support its evolving operations.

Pre-Conference Day, April 21st is dedicated to in-depth learning with SAP experts in interactive all-day workshops. The day concludes with an evening reception in the of the Jacobins Convent in Toulouse where participants can connect with peers in the wonderful atmosphere of the convent which combines history with and unexpected modern flair.

Choose from different topics such as:

  • Energizing the Industry: SAP’s Oil, Gas & Energy Solution Portfolio with SAP's Peter Schlemmer, Kelly Boyer and Robert Davis II. The Oil, Gas, and Energy industry faces price volatility, operational complexity, tightening regulations, and the imperative to accelerate digital transformation. This workshop introduces SAP’s comprehensive solution portfolio designed to address these challenges. Participants will gain insight into how integrated end-to-end processes support operational excellence, explore the solution roadmap for future innovation, and learn best practices that drive efficiency, sustainability, and resilience across the energy value chain.
    The session also highlights SAP’s AI-First, Suite-First strategy combined with the SAP Business Data Cloud, in unlocking new levels of innovation for Oil, Gas, and Energy.
  • Intelligent Service & Asset Management in Energy & Utilities: Powering the Future with Embedded AI with SAP's Hisham Gouda, Susanne Bottemanne, Corey Ritts, and Prakash Karkihallimath. Oil, gas, energy, and utility companies today need a radical rethink of asset lifecycle management—from planning and execution through operations, maintenance, and data-driven insights. This session highlights how SAP’s embedded Enterprise Asset Management (EAM) transforms static, reactive workflows into dynamic, prescriptive, closed-loop processes. You’ll see how EAM integrates AI, IoT, mobile, and analytics into the fabric of asset operations—offering unified, real-time visibility from investment through execution.

Beyond the agenda, the conference offers a range of opportunities to foster connections. 

  • Meet with SAP experts and leadership to discuss current challenges at your company and how to move forward. Please reach out to Janina Crossett to schedule personal meetings in Toulouse.
  • Engage with the community during networking events like the pre-conference evening reception, the Business Women's Networking Lunch, and the legendary celebration evening at the innovative Halle de La Machine.
  • For the first time ever, we are hosting the Energy & Utilities Innovation Awards, honoring global energy leaders driving excellence through innovation and collaboration. The ceremony will take place on the first main conference day, April 21st. 
  • Exchange with experts, partners, and peers during microforums on topics such as Commodity Trading and Risk Management, Business AI in Oil, Gas & Energy, Optimising Oil, Gas, and Energy Downstream Operations, and more.

Explore the event website for more information on the agenda, planned activities, and registration.

We are looking forward to welcoming you to Toulouse to join our mission of connecting minds to power the future.

entry.title: Sales area info not updaing after adding custom tab on Business Partner
entry.content:
Hi everyone,

I have added a custom tab to the Business Partner (BP) transaction in S/4HANA.

I created the tab using the BDT (BUS* transactions) and a Z function group with a custom subscreen containing a table control.

Everything works except for one issue:

My problem is that, when I navigate to my custom tab the standard Sales Area fields in the BP header stop updating correctly (visually).

Here the different scenarios:


1- When I visualize a client starting on some standard tab everything is OK and I can change the sales area.

2- If I go now to my custom tab 'GrypoVend - Marca' the sales area information remains OK. But when I try to change it to another sales org:
Text belonging to the fields change but not the codes.

3- When BP starts directly on my custom tab this Sales area information is empty so I choose one. When I do it so, happens de same as before:
No codes but it does update the description

I want to update the codes too so it all have sense and its correct for the user who sees it. When I debug I get through 
 cvi_bdt_adapter=>get_current_bp_sales_data(
   EXPORTING
   i_table_name = 'KNVV'
   IMPORTING
   e_data_table = gt_knvv[]
   ).

the correct sales area information, but I still want to show it on BP.

Can anyone help me with it or give an explanation/solution?

Regards.

entry.title: Confirm the minimum version of OnPrem S4HANA version required for BRIM Quote Replication iflow
entry.content:
There has been a breaking change for CPQ 2608 regarding the BRIM integration on the iFlow "Replicate Quote 2.0 from SAP CPQ to SAP S/4HANA "

As per the help documentation - https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_CPQ/abe5bf645c9542a5bd4cbfdcc1a4876c/8e920b7b0a2e4d7fa72c306194818522.html, the new iflow "New Replicate Quote 2.0 from SAP CPQ to SAP S/4HANA " will become the default and the old iflow will be deprecated.

After checking the documentation on CPQ BRIM integration, in this page

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_CPQ/a5de4dd4b8b047feb894c618aed793b9/89cca9325a704bd79c39299617c030eb.html

 

It is stated that the minimum version required SAP S/4HANA On-premise release required is OP2023 FPS2.  

But our OnPrem system is on 2021. And S/4 HANA upgrade isn't planned by our Stream leads for 2026 at least.

Could you please confirm if the minimum version of OnPrem S4 HANA version required is correct for the new CPQ BRIMflow?


------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Learn more about the SAP Support user and program here.

entry.title: Re: Confirm the minimum version of OnPrem S4HANA version...
entry.content:
CPQ-BRIM Integration is supported from OP2021 onwards.. In CPQ-BRIM Integration Tiered Pricing feature is supported from OP2023 FPS2 onwards.

The same is present in the help documentation: Setup Tiered Pricing Procedure in SAP S/4HANA | SAP Help Portal

It says here "The SAP S/4HANA will support tiered pricing feature starting from the OP2023 FPS2 release."

 

And in this page System Requirements | SAP Help Portal it says "SAP Billing Revenue and Innovation Management – minimum version 2021 FPS1 release".

entry.title: Re: CLM - Contract and Lease Management Migration in Publ...
entry.content:
Hello @SonyaM

I would not say “this should never exist in any IFRS16 cash flow at any point in time” as a universal accounting statement. I would say that for this migration approach, using Present Value Specified, the setup is not accepted by valuation logic once the specified present value exceeds the remaining contract value used for valuation.

When I said you should verify whether NPV amount was loaded incorrectly, or whether the contract value basis is incomplete, what I meant is that one of these two things may be true:

  1. The liability closing amount taken from RE-FX was loaded correctly, but the remaining lease payments in CLM are incomplete. For example, one or more future lease payment cash flows are missing, or payment term dates are incomplete after cutover. In that case, the contract value in CLM is understated, so the specific present value looks too high only because of the future payments basis is incomplete.
  2. The remaining lease payments in CLM are correct, but the Present Value Specified you imported is not the right value to use for this transition setup. In that case, the Present Value Specified is overstated for the CLM contract basis, even if it matched a report in the legacy system.

That is why reconciliation to legacy is not enough by itself. For the system to valuate the migrated CLM contract successfully, you must align remaining future lease payments in CLM, Present Value Specified, and Asset Default Value. If the liability brought from RE-FX was build on a broader payment basis than the one now represented in CLM, you will get exactly this inconsistency.  

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/3bd9b5b7e36c4628a29ffc464bf69b48/44341a9cb5c74d4dbd3e78b473439ca8.html?locale=en-US

3653143 - Central KBA: Interest Calculation in RE-FX - SAP for Me

On note 3409502 and OBA5, (which is a classic GUI transaction) your observation is correct. If a note requires OBA5, that part is not applicable in Public Cloud, because that transaction is not available there. So, I would not position 3409502 as a practical Public Cloud recommended path for your case.

Best regards

Chris

entry.title: Currency Conversion Integration using SAP CPI with Groovy and XSLT
entry.content:
Introduction

In many real-world integration scenarios, applications need to handle data across different currencies. Whether it’s for reporting, billing, or analytics, converting currency values accurately and in real time is an important requirement.

In this blog, I’ll walk through how I built a simple and practical currency conversion integration using SAP Cloud Integration (CPI). The iFlow accepts a JSON request, calls an external API to fetch exchange rates, processes the response using Groovy, and transforms the output into a clean, readable format using XSLT. Finally, the result is sent as an email, making it easy for end users to view.

 

Integration Flow Overview

This integration flow performs the following:

  • Accepts a currency conversion request in JSON format
  • Calls an external exchange rate API
  • Converts the source amount into target currency
  • Transforms the response into a readable HTML format
  • Sends the final output via Email

 

Integration Flow Diagram

HTTPS Sender → JSON to XML Converter → Content Modifier (Extract Properties) → Request Reply (External API Call) → Groovy Script (Processing) → Content Modifier (Build XML) → XSLT Mapping (HTML Output) → Email Receiver

Below is the iFlow implemented for this scenario:

The iFlow demonstrates message splitting, dynamic routing, and CSV transformation before sending data to respective receivers.

Input Payload Structure

Step 1: JSON to XML Conversion

The incoming JSON payload is converted into XML to enable XPath-based extraction in subsequent steps.

Step 2: Extracting Properties 

Using Content Modifier, the following fields are extracted:

 

 
 
These are stored as exchange properties for reuse.

Step 3: External API Call 

A Request Reply step is used to call the exchange rate API dynamically:

 Step 4: Groovy Script Processing

The API response is processed using Groovy to extract relevant fields and format the converted amount.

 Step 5: Building Output XML

A structured XML is created:

 Step 6: XSLT Transformation

The XML is transformed into an HTML table using XSLT to make the output readable.

 Step 7: Email Notification

 The final formatted result is sent via Email using the Mail adapter.

 

 Challenges Faced 

  • Handling API authentication issues
  • Parsing dynamic JSON responses
  • Resolving null pointer exceptions
  • Ensuring correct data formatting

 

Key Learnings

  • Importance of validating API responses
  • Effective use of Groovy for JSON processing
  • Combining XML and XSLT for formatting
  • Designing efficient CPI iFlows

 

Conclusion

This use case demonstrates how SAP CPI can be leveraged to build real-time integrations involving external APIs, data transformation, and user-friendly output delivery.

This implementation helped me better understand how to design flexible and maintainable integration flows, especially when dealing with real-world requirements.

I hope this experience helps others who are working on similar scenarios in SAP Cloud Integration.

Happy to hear feedback or suggestions from the community.

entry.title: Architecting a Secure API Bridge: Automating SAP Datasphere Data Consumption via OAuth 2.0/Postman
entry.content:
The Strategic Need for an API Bridge

In a modern enterprise, SAP Datasphere acts as the "Single Source of Truth." However, the data stored there often needs to be consumed by diverse downstream consumers:  from custom Python-based AI models and React web applications to third-party reporting engines.

The challenge isn't just "getting the data out"—it’s doing so securely and efficiently. Relying on manual exports or basic authentication creates security risks and operational bottlenecks. By architecting an API Bridge using Postman, I created a standardized, automated, and OAuth 2.0-secured gateway. This allows us to move away from "one-off" data pulls and toward a professional Data-as-a-Service (DaaS) model where any authorized application can consume real-time SAP data on demand.

The blog is divided into 5 parts:

  • Part 1: The Data Foundation (Datasphere - Data Builder)

  • Part 2: Security & Identity—The OAuth 2.0 Client

  •  Part 3: The Connection (Postman Strategy)

    • Option 1: The Manual Approach (Standard)

    • Option 2: The Automated Approach (Professional)

  • Part 4: Real-Time Data Validation (The Visualizer)

  • Part 5: The End Goal—Exporting for Downstream Apps

Part 1: The Data Foundation (Datasphere - Data Builder)

Before an API can "see" your data, it must be properly modeled and exposed. For this setup, I used a dataset of Amazon Bestselling Books.

Why use a Graphical View instead of a Table?

While you can expose a raw table, a Graphical View is the professional choice. It acts as a "Virtual Layer" where you can:

  • Filter Data: Exclude old records so the API doesn't get overloaded.
  • Rename Fields: Change technical headers (like PUB_YR) to business-friendly names (like Publication_Year).
  • Calculations: Add new columns (like Profit_Margin) directly in the view.

Step-by-Step:

  1. Import: Upload your CSV/Table into the Data Builder.
  2. Create View: Drag the table into a new Graphical View.
  3. Semantic Usage: Set this to Relational Dataset (this is mandatory for OData access).
  4. Expose for Consumption: Toggle this ON. This is the most critical step; it tells Datasphere to generate a URL for external tools.
  5. Deploy: Click the "Save and Deploy" icon.

SAP Datasphere Data Builder view properties showing Expose for Consumption toggled on.

 

In the SAP Datasphere Data Builder, ensure your Graphical View is set to 'Relational Dataset' and that 'Expose for Consumption' is toggled ON. A successful deployment (indicated by the green checkmark) is required for the OData service to become reachable.

Part 2: Security & Identity—The OAuth 2.0 Client

To allow Postman to talk to SAP Datasphere, avoid using personal login credentials. Instead, create an OAuth 2.0 Client. This acts as a "Technical User" that is secure, scoped, and easy to manage.

Step 1: Navigate to App Integration

Go to System > Administration > App Integration tab.

SAP Datasphere Data Builder screen showing Model Properties for Bestsellers Books View with Relational Dataset usage and Expose for Consumption toggled ON.

 

Step 2: Create the Technical User

Click on "Add an OAuth Client". In the configuration window:

  1. Provide a Name (e.g., POC_Technical_User).
  2. Set the Authorization Grant to Client Credentials.
  3. Important: Copy your Secret immediately and save it safely; you won't be able to see it again!

Configuration window for an SAP Datasphere OAuth Client showing Name, Client ID field, and the Client Credentials authorization grant type.

 

Step 3: The "403" Safeguard (Role Assignment)

This is the most critical technical step. The API will return a 403 Forbidden error unless permissions are set. Click Select Roles and assign Scoped Data Warehouse Cloud Viewer and Scoped Data Warehouse Cloud Modeler as optional.

Role assignment screen in SAP Datasphere, selecting Scoped Data Warehouse Cloud Modeler and Scoped Data Warehouse Cloud Viewer roles for an OAuth client.

 

Step 4: Note your Endpoints

Once saved, your new client will appear in the Configured Clients list. Make sure to note down the Authorization URL and Token URL shown at the top of the screen. You will need these for your Postman Environment.

SAP Datasphere App Integration screen displaying the configured OAuth Clients list, Authorization URL, and Token URL.

 

 Part 3: The Connection (Postman Strategy)

Now that our SAP Datasphere "Bridge" is built and secured, we need to cross it using Postman. There are two ways to handle the OAuth 2.0 handshake.

Option 1: The Manual Approach (Standard)

While automation is our ultimate goal, establishing a manual connection first is a critical diagnostic step. This ensures that your SAP Datasphere credentials and roles are configured correctly before we add the complexity of scripting. This is the "out-of-the-box" method. It is great for a quick one-time test, but it has a significant drawback: Tokens expire.

Detailed Steps:

Step 1: Configure the Request

In Postman, create a new GET request using your Datasphere OData URL. Navigate to the Authorization tab and select OAuth 2.0. Fill in the "Configure New Token" section using the Client ID and Secret we generated in Part 2.

Postman Authorization tab setup for OAuth 2.0 showing Client ID and Client Secret fields highlighted.

 

Step 2: Authenticate with SAP

Scroll down and click "Get New Access Token". If your credentials are correct, you will see a green checkmark indicating that the authentication handshake was successful.

Postman popup window showing a green checkmark and 'Authentication complete' message.

 

Step 3: Commit the Token

Once authenticated, Postman stores the token in its local manager. You must click "Use Token" to link this specific string to your current request headers.

Postman Manage Access Tokens window highlighting the 'Use Token' button.

 

Headers: Go to the Headers tab. You must add the key x-sap-sac-custom-auth with the value true. This tells the SAP gateway to process your request as a technical user.

 

Step 4: Execute the Call

With the token active, you are ready to hit Send. Postman will now pass the bearer token to SAP Datasphere to request your data.

Postman interface highlighting the blue 'Send' button for the OData request.

Step 5: Validate the Result

A successful connection will return a 200 OK status. You can now see your Bestsellers Books data in raw JSON format. While this confirms the "Bridge" is working, notice how hard it is to read—this is why we will implement the Visualizer in Part 4.

Postman response body showing 200 OK status and raw JSON data for the Amazon Bestseller Books dataset.

The Comparison (Transitioning to Option 2)

The manual method works perfectly, but as you can see, it requires several clicks. If you close Postman or wait an hour, that token will expire, and you will have to repeat these steps.

Next, let's explore how to automate this entire flow using a Pre-request Script, so you never have to click "Get New Token" again.

Option 2: The Automated Approach (Professional)

While the manual approach confirms our connection works, it is not scalable. For a truly professional API Bridge, we need a "Set-and-Forget" solution. By using a Pre-request Script, we turn Postman into an intelligent agent that manages its own OAuth 2.0 lifecycle. This is the "Set-and-Forget" method. We use a Pre-request Script to turn Postman into an intelligent agent that manages its own security.

Step 1: Establishing the Environment Layer

Before we write the script, we must centralize our configuration. Instead of hardcoding URLs and Secrets—which is a security risk and an administrative nightmare—we use Postman Environments.

As shown in the image below, create a new environment and name it (e.g., SAP Datasphere). This allows us to store our ds_url, client_id, and token_url as variables.

Postman Environment tab showing variables for ds_url, client_id, client_secret, token_url, and automated fields for access_token and token_expiry.

Why this matters:

  • Security: Your client_secret is masked.
  • Automation: Notice the access_token and token_expiry variables. Our script will automatically update these values every time a new handshake occurs, keeping the connection "alive" indefinitely.

Step 2: The Pre-request Script (The Brain)

Instead of adding a script to every individual request, we add it to the Collection Folder. This ensures the logic applies to every OData call you ever add to this project.

  1. Click the three dots next to your "Datasphere Connection" collection and select Edit.
  2. Navigate to the Scripts > Pre-request tab.
  3. The Logic: The script checks if a token exists or if the current time has passed the token_expiry. If needed, it fetches a fresh token silently in the background.

Paste this script into the Collection Pre-request tab:

// Check if the token is expired (or doesn't exist)

if (!pm.environment.get("access_token") || Date.now() > pm.environment.get("token_expiry")) {
    console.log("Token expired or missing. Fetching a new one...");
    pm.sendRequest({
        url: pm.environment.get("token_url"),
        method: 'POST',
        header: {
            'Content-Type': 'application/x-www-form-urlencoded',
            'Authorization': 'Basic ' + btoa(pm.environment.get("client_id") + ":" + pm.environment.get("client_secret"))
        },
        body: {
            mode: 'urlencoded',
            urlencoded: [
                { key: 'grant_type', value: 'client_credentials' }
            ]
        }
    }, function (err, res) {
        if (err) {
            console.log("Error fetching token: " + err);
        } else {
            var response = res.json();
            pm.environment.set("access_token", response.access_token);
 
// Set expiry time (current time + expires_in seconds)

            pm.environment.set("token_expiry", Date.now() + (response.expires_in * 1000));
            console.log("New token fetched successfully!");
        }
    });
}

 

Postman Pre-request script showing advanced OAuth 2.0 logic with automated expiration checking and environment variable management.

Click the Save button at the top right of the Collection tab (or press Ctrl+S). If you don't save here, the collection won't "know" to run this logic before your requests.

Step 3: Inherit Authentication

After the Pre-request script fetches a fresh token and saves it to the environment, we need to tell our specific API request to use that value.

  • Authorization: On your GET request, set the Auth Type to Bearer Token.
  • Variable: In the Token field, type {{access_token}}.

           Postman interface showing the 'Bearer Token' authorization type and the reference to the access_token variable.

  • Environment: Ensure the "SAP Datasphere" environment is active in the top-right dropdown.Selecting the correct Environment in the top-right dropdown.
  • Headers Tab: Add the mandatory key 'x-sap-sac-custom-auth' and set it to 'true'. This is essential for both approaches to bypass the SAP login screen.

           Postman Headers tab showing the manually added key set to true, necessary for bypassing interactive SAP login screens during API calls.

  • Send: Click the blue Send button.

What happens when you click Send?

This is the "Grand Opening." When you hit Send, Postman follows this sequence:

  1. The Script Runs: It checks the environment variables. If the token is missing/expired, it calls the SAP Token URL.
  2. Variables Update: You’ll actually see the access_token and token_expiry values change in your Environment tab in real-time.
  3. The GET Request Fires: Postman injects the fresh token into the header and sends the request to Datasphere.
  4. The Result: You receive your 200 OK and the JSON data.

Postman response footer showing a successful 200 OK status code and response time, confirming a secure connection between Postman and SAP Datasphere.

Why this is the "Professional" Way:

This setup creates a seamless link. When you hit Send, Postman follows this sequence:

  1. The Script Runs: It checks the environment variables. If the token is missing/expired, it calls the SAP Token URL.
  2. Variables Update: You’ll actually see the access_token and token_expiry values change in your Environment tab in real-time.
  3. The GET Request Fires: Postman injects the fresh token into the header and sends the request to Datasphere.
  4. The Result: You receive a 200 OK response and the corresponding JSON data.

Benefits:

  • The Automated Advantage: You can walk away for a week, come back, hit "Send," and it will just work. The script handles the handshake before you even realize a token was needed.
  • Self-Healing: If the token expires, the script detects it and fixes it in milliseconds.
  • Security: Your secrets are stored in the Environment layer, not the request itself.
  • Scalability: You can add 100 different OData requests to this collection, and they will all share this automated login logic.

Part 4: Real-Time Data Validation (The Visualizer)

Raw JSON data is hard to read. An alternative option is to use the Postman Visualizer to transform our live SAP Datasphere OData feed into a professional, searchable dashboard.

The "Visualize" Tab:

Step 1: The "Post-Response" Script

In your automated GET request, navigate to the Scripts > Post-response tab. Unlike the Pre-request script, which handles security before the call, this script executes after the data arrives.

Paste the following code to map your OData fields to an HTML table (You can modify the template below as per your need):

// Define the HTML/CSS Template

var template = `
    <style>
        body { font-family: Arial, sans-serif; margin: 20px; }
        #search { margin-bottom: 15px; padding: 10px; width: 100%; border: 1px solid #ccc; border-radius: 4px; }
        table { border-collapse: collapse; width: 100%; }
        th { background-color: #0070f3; color: white; padding: 10px; text-align: left; position: sticky; top: 0; }
        td { border: 1px solid #ddd; padding: 8px; }
        tr:nth-child(even) { background-color: #f2f2f2; }
        tr:hover { background-color: #e9f5ff; }
    </style>
    <input type="text" id="search" onkeyup="searchTable()" placeholder="Search for book names or authors...">
    <table id="bookTable">
        <thead>
            <tr>
                <th>Name</th>
                <th>Author</th>
                <th>Price</th>
                <th>Year</th>
            </tr>
        </thead>
        <tbody>
            {{#each response.value}}
            <tr>
                <td>{{Name}}</td>
                <td>{{Author}}</td>
                <td>{{Price}}</td>
                <td>{{Year}}</td>
            </tr>
            {{/each}}
        </tbody>
    </table>
    <script>
        function searchTable() {
            var input = document.getElementById("search");
            var filter = input.value.toUpperCase();
            var table = document.getElementById("bookTable");
            var tr = table.getElementsByTagName("tr");
            for (var i = 1; i < tr.length; i++) {
                var txtValue = tr[i].textContent || tr[i].innerText;
                if (txtValue.toUpperCase().indexOf(filter) > -1) {
                    tr[i].style.display = "";
                } else {
                    tr[i].style.display = "none";
                }
            }
        }
    </script>
`;

pm.visualizer.set(template, {
    response: pm.response.json()
});

Step 2: The "Grand Opening" (Click Send)

Once your script is saved, click the blue Send button.

  1. Status Check: You will see the 200 OK status return in the bottom right.
  2. Visualize: In the response body section, click the Visualize tab.
  3. Result: Your raw data is now a clean, interactive blue table. You can search for specific authors or titles in real-time.

Postman interface showing the final visualized OData table with a search bar, 200 OK status, and professional blue header formatting.

Part 5: The End Goal—Exporting for Downstream Apps

The ultimate goal of this "Bridge" is to feed other systems without manual intervention.

  • For Business Users: Use Postman’s "Send and Download" feature (click the arrow next to the Send button). This exports the live OData response into a .json or .csv file that can be ingested by Excel or Power BI.

  • For App Developers: Since the OAuth flow is now automated, this Postman Collection can be exported as a JSON file. Developers can use the Postman Collection Runner or Newman to integrate this data feed directly into a CI/CD pipeline or a custom React/Python application.

Conclusion: From Data Silo to API Bridge

In this guide, we didn't just connect Postman to SAP Datasphere—we built a production-ready OData bridge.

  • Exposed: Turned a static View into a live OData service.
  • Secured: Implemented robust OAuth 2.0 scoping.
  • Automated: Used Pre-request scripts to ensure the connection never expires.
  • Validated: Used the Visualizer to prove the data is accurate at a glance.

By following this workflow, you can empower your external applications, third-party vendors, or internal analytics tools with real-time data from your SAP ecosystem—securely and automatically.

#SAPDatasphere #SAPBDC #Postman #OAuth2 #OData #Integration #DataAsAService

entry.title: Dynamic Opening and Closing Balance Calculation in SAP Analytics Cloud Using Script Logic
entry.content:
Introduction 

In SAP Analytics Cloud particularly in financial and treasury reporting often we have a most common requirement to display opening and closing balance based on selected period.

Mostly the requirement will be done by manually adjusting the reporting period or by creating multiple static filters , but in this blog we are going to tackle the requirement by using SAC scripting and input controls. By Filtering the periods before and up to the selected period , SAC can automatically calculate the opening and closing balance and display it in the dashboard.

This blog explains how SAC scripting is used to dynamically show the opening and closing balances based on selected period.

 Business Scenario

The requirement is that the users should view treasury balances based on the selected period. The exact scenario described as following:

When a user chooses a period from dropdown 

  • It should display opening balance ie., it should consider all periods before the user selected period 
  •  It should display closing balance ie., it should consider all periods up to the selected period and 
  •  All other charts should display values only for that selected period.

Approach Used:

SAC scripting is used in OnInitialization event to retrieve selected period and apply necessary changes on the charts to display opening and closing balances based on selected period when the dashboard is loading.

Implementation

The code  written inside the OnInitialization event ensures that filtering happens automatically in the backend.

Step 1 – Dropdown Selection and OnInitialization logic

When the user selects a period from the dropdown, the script retrieves the selected value and applies filters to the charts accordingly.The selected key gets stored in a variable and applied as a filter.

Scriptvariable_Getkey=Dropdown_1.getSelectedKey();
Chart_2.getDataSource().setDimensionFilter("POSTING_YEAR",Scriptvariable_Getkey);

The script then retrieves all members available in the POSTING_YEAR dimension and converts string to numbers to achieve proper comparison.

Scriptvariable_Memberinfo=Chart_2.getDataSource().getMembers("POSTING_YEAR");
Scriptvariable_Number=ConvertUtils.stringToNumber(Scriptvariable_Getkey);

In this step the script identifies all the periods before the selected period to and applies the filtered periods on Chart_2 to retrieve Opening balance.

for (var i = 0; i < Scriptvariable_Memberinfo.length; i++) {
 var year = ConvertUtils.stringToNumber(Scriptvariable_Memberinfo[i].id); 
if (year < Scriptvariable_Number) { Scriptvariable_Filter.push(Scriptvariable_Memberinfo[i].id); 
} 
}
Chart_2.getDataSource().setDimensionFilter("POSTING_YEAR",Scriptvariable_Filter);

To calculate closing balance in this step the script retrieves all the period up to the selected period and applies the filtered period on Chart_3.

for (var l = 0; l < Scriptvariable_Memberinfo.length; l++) { 
var year1 = ConvertUtils.stringToNumber(Scriptvariable_Memberinfo[l].id); 
if (year1 <= Scriptvariable_Number) { Scriptvariable_Filter1.push(Scriptvariable_Memberinfo[l].id);
 } 
}
Chart_3.getDataSource().setDimensionFilter("POSTING_YEAR",Scriptvariable_Filter1);

All other charts are filtered only for the selected period.

Chart_9.getDataSource().setDimensionFilter("POSTING_YEAR",Scriptvariable_Getkey); 
Chart_10.getDataSource().setDimensionFilter("POSTING_YEAR",Scriptvariable_Getkey);

Step 2 – Default Behaviour on Page Load

Initially when the dashboard gets loaded in dropdown it shows the current year and month will be in the dropdown to enhance usability.The below script retrieves current month and year and formats accordingly.

Scriptvariable_CurrentYear = (new Date()).getFullYear().toString(); Scriptvariable_Currentmonth=(new Date()).getMonth().toString();
ScriptVariable_CurrentmonthNum=ConvertUtils.stringToInteger(Scriptvariable_Currentmonth); ScriptVariable_CurrentmonthNum1=ScriptVariable_CurrentmonthNum+1; ScriptVariable_Currentmonthstr=ConvertUtils.numberToString(ScriptVariable_CurrentmonthNum1);
ScriptVariable_Currentmonthstr1=('0'+ScriptVariable_Currentmonthstr).slice(-2);
Scriptvariable_Combine=Scriptvariable_CurrentYear+ScriptVariable_Currentmonthstr1;

 The below script ensures that users always see the recent financial data immediately after opening the dashboard.

Dropdown_1.setSelectedKey(Scriptvariable_Combine);

Conclusion:

Dynamic reporting is necessary especially for a financial dashboard where the requirement is to automatically show opening and closing balances without any manual workload . SAC scripting approach simplifies the process and improves efficiency and enhances the user experience.

Output :

 Dynamic Dropdown for Period-Based Filtering

Dynamic Balance Calculation Based on Selected Period

entry.title: Dynamic Filtering in SAC using Logged in user details to show organization level data
entry.content:
Introduction :

In SAC a common requirement is to filter data automatically based on the logged-in user. SAC makes this relatively straightforward because the logged-in User ID can be retrieved directly through scripting.

In our scenario, the requirement was slightly different users should not see only their own records, but rather all the records belonging to their organization. To achieve this requirement we need to first get the organization details of the logged in user then pass it as a filter across the story.

This blog will explain how this requirement shall be implemented using scripting in SAC and hidden helper charts.

Business requirement :

The dashboard is used by users from multiple organizations.

So when the user opens the dashboard:

  1. The user information need to be identified.
  2. Then we need to find the organization associated with the user.
  3. The dashboard should display all the records related to the users organization.
  4. User should be able to see the records related to other users of the same organization.

    Approach used:

    You may think why can’t we directly filter by user ID for this scenario, we could filter the dashboard using the user ID but doing that it would only show the data related to that particular user we won’t be able to show the records of all other users of the same organization.

    So to achieve this,

    1. We first get the logged in user ID.
    2. Retrieve the organization associated with the user.
    3. Apply that organization as filter to the dashboard.
    4. In addition to that we have also dynamically populated the dropdown based on the filtered data.

      To achieve this we shall use 2 helper charts in the story. These charts will be hidden in the dashboard and will be only used to retrieve data through scripting.

      Chart 1 - is used to retrieve the organization associated with the user.

      Chart 2 : Retrieves the user belonging to the filtered organization.

      Code :

      The code is written in the onIntialization event of the SAC story

      1. Retrieve the ID of the user and pass it as filter on Chart 1 to retrieve the relevant records.

var1 = Application.getUserInfo().id;
Chart_1.getDataSource().setDimensionFilter("PR_CREATED_BY",var1);

2. After applying the filter we retrieve the result set and get the user organization.

var2 = Chart_1.getDataSource().getResultSet();
for (var i = 0; i < var2.length; i++) {
var3.push(var2[i]["Organisation"].id);
}

3. Populate the dropdown

Dropdown_1.removeAllItems();
for (var j = 0; j < var3.length; j++) {
Dropdown_1.addItem(var3[j]);
}

4. Applying the organization filter to the table and retrieving the user based on organization.

Table_4.getDataSource().setDimensionFilter("Organisation", var3);
Chart_2.getDataSource().setDimensionFilter("Organisation",var3);
var5 = Chart_2.getDataSource().getResultSet();
for (var k = 0; k < var5.length; k++) {
var6.push(var5[k]["PR_CREATED_BY"].id);
}

Therefore through the above approach the user can see the organisation level details.

Output Scenarios :
1. When one user is associated with more than one organization all those organization will get populated in the dropdown and same will be shown in table


2. The dashboard displays all records belonging to the user’s organization, allowing users to view data created by other users within the same organization.



3. Hidden charts that are used to fetch user organization and user id details

entry.title: How to calculate the date difference between 2 rows of same column in Manage dashboards of SAP DM
entry.content:
Hi,

For one of my dashboard requirement, i need to calculate the date difference between two events but the date appears of 2 event in same column but in 2 different rows. for example, i want to calculate the difference between hold and resume time of SFC. SFC production events MDO has this time in EVentoccuredat column but as 2 rows differentiated by Eventtype as "SFC_HOLD" and "SFC_HOLD_RELEASED". does anyone have an idea to calculate this duration.

 

Thanks

MS
